Home / Blog

A different way, Part 1

April 19, 2026

This is going to be a bit of a different post. If the topics of religion and spirituality make you uncomfortable, I encourage you to read on anyway. Sit in the discomfort, and feel around to understand why you are uncomfortable. Is your heart or soul or mind trying to tell you something?

Last week (the week of April 5th) was Eastern Orthodox Holy Week. I decided to take that week off from work, and the result was an unexpected reflection on my life and what is important to me. That inspired this post, which is part one of a two (or maybe more) part series.

I was raised in the Greek Orthodox church, a part of the family of Eastern Orthodox churches. For the uninitiated, Eastern Orthodoxy is the other branch of Christianity that resulted from the East-West schism. The "Western" church contains Catholicism and all other denominations you are probably familiar with. Unfortunately, throughout my life I have seen how Christianity has gotten a bad rap. And in some sense, that's probably justified. It has been used to justify wars, conquest, and massacres have been committed by warring sects. More recently, there have been numerous child sexual abuse reports and scandals in the Catholic church especially, even one reported as recently as early March of this year, where a multiyear investigation revealed that dozens of priests in Rhode Island molested hundres of victims over decades. Child sexual abuse happens even in the Eastern Orthodox Church, but it receives much less media attention in the west. A post on the Orthodox Church of America website is remarkably candid about this.

Perhaps the most abundant criticism of Christianity nowadays is that it asks its believers to believe unbelievable things, such as that a man lived who was also God and also the Son of God and died by crucifixion and rose from the dead three days later, appearing to his followers. This is a lot to accept, not least because it is entirely incompatible with our modern understanding of reality. I think I speak for many of us, however, in saying that we all still wonder if there is something more beyond our day-to-day rhythm of work, make money, pay bills, watch Netflix, sleep.

Despite the dogma of the church, which I certainly have a difficult time accepting most of the time, I still remain active in my Orthodox church. This Holy Week, I reflected on why, and the answers boiled down to the following,

  1. I have an intuitive recognition of the presence of God in my life.
  2. I somehow understand that the teachings of Jesus offer a way to live without anxiety or fear, in a way that is beautiful and selfless. My heart resonates with these teachings as the right way to live, as opposed to trying to hoard as much money as possible to become materially wealthy, and conceited and selfish as a result.
  3. I have a deeply rooted community of friends that I grew up with and still stay in touch with in the church. More on this in the second part of this series.

This line of thinking then led me to wonder why the Catholic church does not have this same allure (or at least it doesn't seem to when I talk to people about their experiences in it). Both the Orthodox and Catholic traditions both date back centuries and are nominally quite similar in their theologies. There is an important difference, however, in how God is experienced. I feel that this excerpt from Wikipedia on the East-West Schism puts it quite well :

These doctrinal issues center around the Orthodox perception that the Catholic theologians lack the actual experience of God called theoria and thereby fail to understand the importance of the heart as a noetic or intuitive faculty. It is what they consider to be the Catholic Church's reliance on pagan metaphysical philosophy and rational methods such as scholasticism rather than on the intuitive experience of God (theoria) that causes Orthodox to consider the Catholic Church heretical.

In other words, Catholics and the denominations that derive therefrom do not put an actual mystical, spiritual experience at the forefront of their teaching. Instead, some modern churches, from the stories I've heard, seem like glorified singalongs that try to induce a quasi-spiritual emotional state on the individual level, without the open-heartedness that would be required for deep spiritual contemplation.

As a member of the Orthodox Church, on the other hand, I have always had an intuitive notion that there is something more. A feeling. It isn't just about the unique opulence or the sensory experience you get when entering an Orthodox place of worship. It's not lust, and it's not desire fulfilled, as a smoker might achieve with a puff of the day's first cigarette. It's the ability to tap into a reservoir of stillness.

Perhaps you might have encountered this feeling before when you have become completely engulfed in a creative activity. You lose yourself in it. You may have encountered it when spending time with really good friends. You lose yourself in happiness. I call it stillness because in these states, the mind does not wander, and it does not want. It is peace in the ultimate sense. Achieving this stillness of mind through meditative prayer is the goal of Eastern Orthodox religious practice.

This practice is not very different from other world religions that emphasize nonattachment, presence in the moment, and meditative states of consciousness, such as Buddhism and Taoism.

In Christ the Eternal Tao, Hieromonk Damascene of the St. Herman of Alaska monastery in the mountains of northern California explores the intuitive way in which the Tao Teh Ching essentially describes the Orthodox God and Christ.1 Recall that Taoism was developed before the time of the historical Jesus. We all have something universal written on our hearts. To discover it, all we need to do is push aside all of the distractions of the world and listen. As a taste of this spiritual contemplation, here is an excerpt of the Tao Teh Ching placed in the book's epigraph,2

There exists a Being undifferentiated and complete,
Born before heaven and earth.
Tranquil, boundless,
Abiding alone and changing not,
Encircling everything without exhaustion.
Fathomless, it seems to be the Source of all things.
I do not know its name,
But characterize it as the Tao.
Arbitrarily forcing a name upon it,
I call it Great....

It is not difficult to experience Tao. Consider how the tree branches all move individually when struck by a breeze, and yet the tree is also dancing. Look around at your greatest friends, and reflect on how they came into your lives. I'll bet it was more-or-less by accident. The warmth of the sun, heating and invigorating all things. Cold, warmth, darkness, light, movement, stillness. The plants and animals that exist for us to marvel at, and yet we disregard them. When reflecting on such things, I fill up with awe and gratitude, and I hope you do too. But I digress.

Western Christianity, on the other hand, preoccupies itself with measuring the moral "weight" of sins and good works, and justifying its followers through aphorisms like "sola fide"3 and "sola scriptura,"4.

To me, all these little tweaks and distortions are rationalizations made by followers who wanted to justify why they would be "going to heaven" without having to put in the work of being good humans on this Earth. Even a cursory glance at the Bible invalidates a lot of these claims.

All this is to say, I believe that despite what many people say about Christianity, there is value in its teachings, especially those that are more commonly emphasized in Eastern Orthodoxy. The truths that Christianity tried to convey—but the Western tradition in some cases has strayed from—are fundamental, and more universally accessible to all people than one might think. And they are profoundly relevant.

Why not contemplate them?

Why not explore questions about the ultimate?

Let's face it. The world is in need of spiritual guidance. The United States is fighting yet another fruitless war. Depression and anxiety among young people have skyrocketed, while Big Tech rakes in increasing amounts of money each year, profiting off that anxiety by encouraging social comparison and addiction through algorithms. AI is killing our ability to think.

Simply going about our days as usual will not solve these problems. Our daily routines have already been molded by this machine. Questions of spirituality can thus be viewed as questions of action. How can we effect positive change on the world? What can we do to shield ourselves from the slings and arrows of the world that draw our minds away from stewardship of the world?

How to save the world

All of us have probably thought at one point or another about how to build a better world. To find evidence of this, consider how many people fervently subscribe to political parties, each of which publishes exhaustive documentation of their platform. A platform is essentially a plan to save the world.

Despite a common desire to build a better world, many online thinkers, writers, and news pundits consistently belabor the point that we cannot have open dialogue anymore. There is truth to this though. Many people literally cannot tolerate disagreement, because we have ideologically sorted ourselves into echo chambers, enabled by the virtualization5 of communication on the internet. A wedge has been driven between the sets of values to which each group holds dear.

A new set of unifying principles is needed to dislodge this wedge. I say "new" because for many centuries, money has been the unifying principle. Money is unifying in the sense that it enables practical coordination between people independently of what language they might speak or where they originate from. In order for money to have power, though, we had to internalize a whole host of assumptions. For example, "the purpose of life is to generate economic value, for which I will be rewarded with the resources to command that others produce economic value for me," or "labor is an instrument at my disposal, given money."

In the rest of this post, I will push back against these generally accepted assumptions, and offer new unifying principles inspired by what really matters.

Crucifying the old principles

The language of money is universally spoken, but it is not universally expressive. Unpriced externalities, such as environmental side effects of the economic engine, are by definition unpriced. The cancer burden is projected to keep growing, with harmful chemicals in our supply chain likely playing a significant role. There is mounting evidence of associations between pesticide use and certain cancers; even the way we produce food harms us. Big Tech profits immensely from harvesting our attention through algorithmically curated infinite-scroll media to serve our brains as many advertisements as humanly possible, but a growing body of research suggests that this has been severely destructive to our attention spans, formation of narrative identity in adolescence, learning outcomes, literacy, and the ability to have spiritual experiences.6 7

Money has largely been a losing bet for our society. It provides a cold, calculated interface for exchanging utility between humans, but in exchange we give up what we actually live for: connection, love, warmth. This is not to say that free enterprise is inherently bad; rather, the pursuit of money—through enterprise or otherwise—has blinded us to the needs of our neighbors. Instead of freely giving and freely receiving, a transactional pattern has been imposed onto all things—think "I will only help you if you deserve it or I am compensated." As Rabbi Michael Knopf points out, one midrash on the Tower of Babel posits that the error of the people building the Tower of Babel was this very same inversion:

... the people were so passionate about building the tower, so convinced were they of its utter necessity, that they paid no mind if a worker on the tower fell to his death; whereas if a brick fell, they would wail and mourn and lament the setback. The peoples’ conviction of the justness of their cause, aided and abetted by the universality of their belief, diminished their humanity and their concern for the welfare of their fellow human beings.

Our economy is the new Tower. And the latest tier of the tower is nearly complete, in the form of artificial intelligence. AI has the potential to manifest immense good, and this is the version of the future I hope to push for and will continue to argue for in my writings. However, if additional tiers of the tower continue to be built, we may invite the wrath of God (literally, or figuratively, whichever interpretation you prefer) who wants us to be proud of our individuality rather than conformity in pursuit of a single-minded (and absent-minded) goal.

Jesus also recognized the perverseness of having money as the fabric of society. A classic example of this can be found in all four canonical gospels. Jesus' entry into the temple and subsequent outrage at those buying and selling within. Here is the version from Mark 11:15-19,

Then they came to Jerusalem. And he entered the temple and began to drive out those who were selling and those who were buying in the temple, and he overturned the tables of the money changers and the seats of those who sold doves, and he would not allow anyone to carry anything through the temple. He was teaching and saying, “Is it not written, ‘My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations’? But you have made it a den of robbers.”

Jesus is explicitly condemning those who exploit others with money. This is the same exploitation that happens each and every day billions of times over on Earth today. In America, our clothes are bought with borderline slave labor in Southeast Asia. Our chocolate is bought with child labor in Africa.

This ethos of rejection of money as a mediator of value is illustrated further in how the early followers, the first people who can be called Christians, conducted themselves in the book of Acts. I have always found the early chapters of Acts to be particularly heartwarming, because they describe a state of living in communion with each other and their surroundings. For example,8

They devoted themselves to the apostles’ teaching and to fellowship, to the breaking of bread and to prayer. Everyone was filled with awe at the many wonders and signs performed by the apostles. All the believers were together and had everything in common. They sold property and possessions to give to anyone who had need. Every day they continued to meet together in the temple courts. They broke bread in their homes and ate together with glad and sincere hearts, praising God and enjoying the favor of all the people. And the Lord added to their number daily those who were being saved.

And there's more,9

All the believers were one in heart and mind. No one claimed that any of their possessions was their own, but they shared everything they had. With great power the apostles continued to testify to the resurrection of the Lord Jesus. And God’s grace was so powerfully at work in them all that there were no needy persons among them. For from time to time those who owned land or houses sold them, brought the money from the sales and put it at the apostles’ feet, and it was distributed to anyone who had need. Joseph, a Levite from Cyprus, whom the apostles called Barnabas (which means “son of encouragement”), sold a field he owned and brought the money and put it at the apostles’ feet.

Worldly possessions are sold, and proceeds are allocated to whomever has need. In this community, ownership does not exist. The disciples' way of life sent ripples out into the world, and inspired many to give up things that we have been taught to value, chief among them money, and even including societal convention. It is Christians' hope that the actions of followers of Christ animate us similarly.

We are thus called to a different way. We need to get our moral story straight, lest we continue down the path of building the Tower and allowing our collective morality be shaped by consumerism and advertisements, and the "hustle" to keep the engine turning, which wants to create in us an endless hunger for cheap goods whose manufacturing slowly destroys the planet with which we were endowed.10

I think we can straighten out that moral story by remembering that we are all cut from the same cloth. In order to find principles that unifying us, let us turn inward, and interrogate what ultimately matters to all people.

Unifying principles

I entreat you all to consider this question deeply: what will you have to say for yourself when you shuffle off this mortal coil?

Our modern culture likes to ignore the ugly fact that we all must die. People like Bryan Johnson are trying to find ways to prevent it. Perhaps these efforts will pan out, but I am not too hopeful. There is a part of me that hopes people like him do find a way to escape death, but I also see death as the great equalizer. It is a reminder that the material is ultimately worthless. This is a unifying principle.

If you have experienced the death of a family member or friend, think about how you remember them. You probably don't remember their possessions, or their wealth. Rather, you remember how they treated you and their character as a person. Therefore, you will also be remembered by your character, and what you contributed, rather than what you had or took away (you may be remembered negatively for those latter things).

And besides, can living in order to not die truly be called life? Stillness of mind and spirit come when we accept impermanence. In Tibet, there is a unique custom where before going to sleep each night, a person flips over a glass on their night stand. This little ritual serves as a reminder that tomorrow might not come. It represents a clean break from life, letting go of earthly attachments to prepare for that possibility. And if tomorrow does come, then the person rejoices that they can see sunlight and hear birds chirping, and flips the glass right-side-up.

This daily ritual of detachment and reattachment is the opposite of how we usually live our lives according to weekly, monthly, and yearly plans. According to the Gospels, Jesus repeatedly made this point as well. Rich people hoard their wealth, people pass by others in need on the street without even looking at them, and religious officials (Pharisees) enforce laws to ensure eveyone is performing the part of the good citizen while under the covers they do not respect the law. People make excuses or assume that they'll have tomorrow to make amends.

Now consider that much stress in life comes from worries about the future, or embarrassment of the past. Jesus calls us to move on from these things. The future and past essentially do not exist. We can see, hear, touch, smell, and affect the present, and the present alone. We can find a unifying principle in the inevitability of death, and also in the reality of impermanence.

Jesus' words speak directly to these principles. 11 12

“Therefore I tell you, do not worry about your life, what you will eat or drink; or about your body, what you will wear. Is not life more than food, and the body more than clothes?” “Look at the birds of the air; they do not sow or reap or store away in barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not much more valuable than they?” “Can any one of you by worrying add a single hour to your life?”

Or as the late, great Robin Williams' character in Dead Poet's Society13 says, carpe diem! Seize the day! When someone needs your help, act immediately. When you feel remorse for something you did in the past, make amends immediately. It should feel intuitive that this is the pressure release valve for our consciences.

The necessity of action is a unifying principle. Nothing happens in our lives until we choose action.

If you live in the west, when you hear Christianity, you might think of the associated controversial political stances, or its insistence that Christians believe in what some might call fantasies. At the same time, you might also imagine that society would be better if people owned fewer things and material possessions were distributed equitably, precisely as the followers of Jesus behaved in Acts.

The desire for a better world, then, is a unifying principle.

If you have not thought much about religious or spiritual life, or have negative views towards Christianity, can you at least see how there is value in Jesus' words?

It seems that some young people are starting to spontaneously answer this question in the affirmative. Anecdotes recently have been emerging about renewed interest in participating in religious "third places." However, at large, the dechurching trend in America is stark. Jake Meador sharply diagnoses cuts at the problem I'm getting at,

Contemporary America simply isn’t set up to promote mutuality, care, or common life. Rather, it is designed to maximize individual accomplishment as defined by professional and financial success. Such a system leaves precious little time or energy for forms of community that don’t contribute to one’s own professional life or, as one ages, the professional prospects of one’s children. Workism reigns in America, and because of it, community in America, religious community included, is a math problem that doesn’t add up.

I'll take what Meador suggests one step further: America is the place in the world most afflicted by the substitution of human care and connection with transactional relationships. Therefore, we are most in need of a different way.

If nothing else, take away from this piece that we are unified by

  1. inevitability of death
  2. impermanence of the present, nonexistence of the past and future
  3. selfless action as spiritual currency
  4. the desire for a better world

Love God, and your neighbor, and the way will become clear.

Concluding thoughts

The different way is characterized by activity, as opposed to passivity. Vigilance as opposed to indifference. Courage as opposed to fear.14

In other words, the different way is the ability to rewrite your own story. In writing that, I am reminded of the beautiful poem O Me! O Life! by Walt Whitman, which I'll plop in here in its entirety because it deserves to be,

Oh me! Oh life! of the questions of these recurring,
Of the endless trains of the faithless, of cities fill’d with the foolish,
Of myself forever reproaching myself, (for who more foolish than I, and who

     more faithless?)

Of eyes that vainly crave the light, of the objects mean, of the struggle ever 

     renew’d,

Of the poor results of all, of the plodding and sordid crowds I see around

     me,

Of the empty and useless years of the rest, with the rest me intertwined,
The question, O me! so sad, recurring—What good amid these, O me, O 

     life?


                                      Answer.

That you are here—that life exists and identity,
That the powerful play goes on, and you may contribute a verse.

I do not intend for this post to be political or offensive. My aim is simply to critique the typical mode of going through life on autopilot, or with the illusion of not being on autopilot—when one has goals in mind that were "given" to them through social comparison or fear or jealousy—and to invite us to apply our critical thinking skills. Any line of sufficiently critical thought, I think, arrives at the frivolity of the pursuit of money and status and all other vainglorious things.

I'm sure this won't land for everyone, and that's okay. But if it lands for you, drop me an email and subscribe to my newsletter with the box at the bottom of the page. One of my goals with this blog is to start a community around this sort of thinking.

There is a lot more to say on this subject, which is why I want to make this a multi-part series. My good friend Nick and I have decided to hold each other accountable to posting on our blogs every two weeks. Nick, looking forward to seeing your post up soon ;).

In the next part, I will recount a particularly moving experience from my adolescence which revealed to me the boundless love that can exist in social bonds between humans. Then, I will reflect on how modern churches may be turning into "just in case" religion, and propose an alternative path forward.

  1. In all honesty, I have only read the introduction of this book. It deserves a full read, and I'll probably have a lot more to write about when I do read it. ↩
  2. "Tao Teh Ching", Chapters 25 and 4 ↩
  3. "Faith alone," the Protestant Reformation doctrine that one only needs faith to be justified before God; it doesn't matter what works you do while on Earth. ↩
  4. "scripture alone," the Protestant Reformation doctrine that the Bible is infallible and the final authority for Christian practice. ↩
  5. as opposed to having to satisfy social needs with people close to you physically, regardless of their viewpoints; physical proximity is not necessarily related to ideological proximity ↩
  6. I don't have any citations for "ability to have spiritual experiences" because evidence for this is sparse in scientific literature. I imagine it is something that's hard to study empirically, because spiritual experience is subjective and highly variable per individual. I let my intuition guide me in writing this; make of it what you will. For an interesting but somewhat unrelated read on how attentional fatigue and ↩
  7. Another interesting read related to attention, attentional fatigue, and the difference between directed attention and involuntary attention or "fascination" is "The Restorative Benefits of Nature: Toward an Integrative Framework" by Stephen Kaplan. Fascination with nature is deeply intertwined, in my view, with movements of the spirit. Here's an excerpt: "Nature is certainly well-endowed with fascinating objects, as well as offering many processes that people find engrossing. Many of the fascinations afforded by the natural setting qualify as 'soft' fascinations: clouds, sunsets, snow patterns, the motion of the leaves in the breeze-these readily hold the attention, but in an undramatic fashion. Attending to these patterns is effortless, and they leave ample opportunity for thinking about other things." Kaplan then goes on to reference Olmstead, the famous American landscape architect, who "was particularly sensitive to the role of 'natural scenery' in restoration: it 'employs the mind without fatigue and yet exercises it; tranquilizes it and yet enlivens it; and thus, through the influence of the mind over the body, gives the effect of refreshing rest and reinvigoration to the whole system.'" This sounds quite spiritual to me, so I hypothesize there's a deep connection. Time spent scrolling on a phone or computer is obviously time not spent attending in an "undramatic way" to nature, so the link seems pretty plausible. This excessively long footnote is probably a smell that I should expound on this further in a future post. ↩
  8. Acts 2:42-47 ↩
  9. Acts 4:32-37 ↩
  10. I plan on talking more about this process of moral corruption through a hypothesis I have around the repeated juxtaposition of inconsistent concepts in a future post. ↩
  11. Matthew 6:12 ↩
  12. Matthew 6:25-27 ↩
  13. Moving and wonderful movie, highly recommend. ↩
  14. A theme that keeps arising for me in contemplation of such things is the relationship between things and their opposites. The One and the Other. X and not X. Plenty more to meditate on here. Perhaps this will lend itself to some esoteric posts on here in the future. ↩
